coredata.dc:title: Development of highly selective novel fluorescence turn-on probe based on carbon dots for sensing peroxynitrite (ONOO−) in vitro and in vivo
coredata.dc:creator: Abdulkhaleq, Ahmed Mohammed Ali
Zhao, Yixu
Qian, Kun
Wang, Xueshi
Zuo, Zhongfu
Zhou, Xibin
coredata.dc:description: Peroxynitrite (ONOO−), a highly reactive nitrogen and oxygen species (RNS/ROS), has been associated with a numerous of pathophysiological diseases, including inflammation, cancer, neurodegenerative, and cardiovascular diseases. There are great demands to develop strategies that can detect ONOO− in biological systems. Herein, we developed a novel fluorescent probe based on carbon dots (CDs) for a very sensitive and selective detection and imaging of endogenous and exogenous ONOO− in cells and animal models. Green light-emitting (527 nm) CDs with high quantum yield (QY) was synthesized by hydrothermal method using resorcinol as carbon source. After the phenolic hydroxyl groups of the CDs are combined with the recognition group of ONOO− via covalent ether bond, the fluorescence is quenched and forming the fluorescent probe. Upon the addition of ONOO− to the probe, the ether bond has broken and the CDs fluorescence has recovered. This response has been detailed investigated, including response time, pH values, selectivity and sensitivity, and linearity range, which confirmed the satisfactory detection ability for ONOO−. The probe shows low cytotoxicity and was successfully applied for imaging of ONOO− exogenously and endogenously in cells and in live models of zebrafish and mice.

(d) Fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths of CDs from 408 nm to 518 nm (with an interval of 10 nm). Design of CDs-O-PDOB for ONOO− detection.

head.ce:title: Development of highly selective novel fluorescence turn-on probe based on carbon dots for sensing peroxynitrite (ONOO−) in vitro and in vivo

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Peroxynitrite (ONOO−), a highly reactive nitrogen and oxygen species (RNS/ROS), has been associated with a numerous of pathophysiological diseases, including inflammation, cancer, neurodegenerative, and cardiovascular diseases. There are great demands to develop strategies that can detect ONOO− in biological systems. Herein, we developed a novel fluorescent probe based on carbon dots (CDs) for a very sensitive and selective detection and imaging of endogenous and exogenous ONOO− in cells and animal models. Green light-emitting (527 nm) CDs with high quantum yield (QY) was synthesized by hydrothermal method using resorcinol as carbon source. After the phenolic hydroxyl groups of the CDs are combined with the recognition group of ONOO− via covalent ether bond, the fluorescence is quenched and forming the fluorescent probe. Upon the addition of ONOO− to the probe, the ether bond has broken and the CDs fluorescence has recovered. This response has been detailed investigated, including response time, pH values, selectivity and sensitivity, and linearity range, which confirmed the satisfactory detection ability for ONOO−. The probe shows low cytotoxicity and was successfully applied for imaging of ONOO− exogenously and endogenously in cells and in live models of zebrafish and mice.

A novel fluorescence turn-on probe based on carbon dots was designed and successfully synthesized for the detection of peroxynitrite (ONOO−) in vitro and in vivo.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Peroxynitrite (ONOO−) is known as a highly reactive nitrogen and oxygen species (RNS/ROS), playing a significant role in several physiological and pathological processes [1–3]. ONOO− is formed from the diffusion-controlled reaction of superoxide radicals (O2 •−) and nitric oxide (NO) in living systems [4–6]. ONOO− has roles in signal transduction, wherein it can react uncontrollably with various biomolecules due to its strong oxidizing and nitrating characteristics [7–9]. High levels of ONOO− in the human body have been associated with inflammatory diseases [10,11], cardiovascular disorders [12], Alzheimer's disease [13], Parkinson's disease [14], pulmonary [15], renal [16] and hepatic injuries [17], as well as cancer [18]. Consequently, it is of great importance to sensitively and selectively detect the existence of ONOO− in living organisms, understand its physiological functions, and analyze related diseases. Up to date, various traditional analytical methods have been established for ONOO− detection, including chromatography [19], electrochemical approaches [20], capillary electrophoresis [21], electron spin resonance [22], and so on. However, these techniques are high-cost and require sophisticated instruments and trained operators [23]. Therefore, it is of great interest to develop a simple, rapid, low-cost, and sensitive analytical method for ONOO− detection.

Some current methods are predominantly based on developing fluorescent probes. Fluorescent probes generally are critical for understanding various intracellular processes and give a capable explanatory method for clarifying the part of active substances over living framework, due to their good sensitivity, high selectivity, rapid reaction time, in vivo real-time detection, and minimum equipment technical requirements [24–28]. Most of these fluorophores are based on fluorescent organic dyes [29,30], metal complexes [31], nanocrystals [32], and carbon dots (CDs) [33–35]. Recently, many fluorescent probes for the detection of ONOO− have been reported [36–41]. Despite this great progress, some problems such as the complexity of organic molecule synthesis, weak constancy to other ROS/RNS interference, and the need to improve the fluorescence stability still exist [42,43]. Consequently, it is necessary to develop a probe with high cellular permeability, preferable selectivity and reactivity profiles, and low cytotoxicity to detect endogenous ONOO− produced during pathophysiological conditions. This has encouraged us to design and synthesize a new fluorescent probe based on a low-cost fluorophore with a facile synthesis approach, good fluorescence activity, and compatible with living cells for sensitively and selectively visualizing ONOO−.

Carbon dots (CDs) have received wide attention from researchers due to their high fluorescence quantum yield, unique surface properties, excellent solubility and stability, and low cytotoxicity [44–46]. CDs are typically defined as small carbon nanoparticles with a size of less than 10 nm. Their structures possess functional groups on the surface of carbon cores which can be functionalized by selected chemicals for targeting various intracellular biomolecules [47]. The interactions between carbon dots and chemicals can produce Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [48], photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [49], intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [50], and inner filter effect (IFE) [51] leading to the quenching of the fluorescence properties of carbon dots. Many researchers have developed CDs-based fluorescent probes for the detection of various biomolecules. For example, J. Zhang et al. and J. Sun et al. have reported fluorescent probes based on CDs for the detection of biothioles in living cells [49,52]. B. Liu et al. has designed nitrogen-doped CDs as a fluorescent probe for the detection of folic acid [53]. Q. Wang et al. has prepared fluorescent probe from CDs and applied it for the imaging of Selenol in living cells [54]. According to our understanding, the relatively few reports about CDs-based fluorescent probes for the detection of ONOO− have been developed. J. Shao et al. and coworkers have reported a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on CDs and Phycocyanin (PC) for the detection of ONOO− but not applied to biological system [55]. Y. Meng et al. [56] and J. Zhu et al. [57] has reported the CDs-based system for the detection of ONOO− through “turn-off” response, respectively. Compare to “turn-off” mode, “turn-on” probes are excelled due to their high efficiency, high signal-to-noise ratio, and ingrained sensitivity [58–60].

Herein, we designed and synthesized a new CDs-derived fluorescent probe for ONOO− turn-on detection (Scheme 1 ). The CDs is prepared by a simple hydrothermal method with high quantum yield (QY). The reaction of preparing the probe designed to conjugate the CDs through ether bond with 2-[4-(bromomethyl)phenyl]-4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolane (Br-PDOB) as the recognition group, which has been reported to possess a fast reaction with ONOO− [17,50,61,62]. This highly performed fluorescent probe with its high sensitivity, good selectivity, and low cytotoxicity was suitable for visualizing ONOO− in biological systems.

All chemicals were obtained from commercial supplies and they are of an appropriate grade for laboratory use. Resorcinol (C6H6O2) was obtained from Shanghai Saen Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. 2-(4-(Bromomethyl)phenyl)-4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolane (Br-PDOB) was obtained from Shanghai Bide Pharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was obtained from Tianjin Heowns Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was obtained from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Methanol, Dichloromethane (DCM) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was purchased from Tianjin Bailun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. N,N-diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA), amino acids, hydrogen peroxide, and glucose were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Various inorganic salts were obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. Na2HPO4.12H2O and KH2PO4 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Silica gel powder was obtained from Qingdao Haiyang Chemical Industry. All of the chemicals were utilized as received without further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the synthesis and analysis processes.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) measurements were performed on (TEM, Talos F200X, FEI, USA). The x-ray diffraction (XRD) results were measured using an X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, D-8, Bruker-axs, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on an (IRAffinity-1, Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured by (Kratos Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer (Aligent Company, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a Cary 50 (Varian Company, USA). Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra was performed on a Harp 300 system (PicoQuant, Germany). The absolute quantum yield (QY) was measured with a Fluoromax-4 (HORIBA, USA). The cellular fluorescence images were captured using a Cytation 5 Cell Imaging Multi-Mode Reader (BioTek, USA). The in-vivo mouse imaging experiments were performed on Bruker Image Station System (Bruker, USA).

CDs was prepared according to previously reported method with modifications [63,64]. Resorcinol (0.4 g) and sulfuric acid (0.4 mL) were dissolved properly in 10 mL ultrapure water after sonication for 5 min and the homogeneous solution was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL) followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the reactor spontaneously cooled to room temperature naturally, and then the pH of the solution was neutralized by adding sodium hydroxide. The neutralized precipitate was then washed with water and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min three times to remove water-soluble impurities. After that, the supernatant was dried and the CDs was finally obtained as a dark brown powder.

For the synthesis of the probe (Scheme 1), the CDs (100 mg) was dissolved in dry DMF (6 mL) with the aid of sonication, then 2-(4-(Bromomethyl)phenyl)-4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolane (175 mg) and N,N-diisopropylethylamine (100 μL) were added to the solution and stirred vigorously by using a magnetic stirrer in an oil bath at 45 °C overnight. After that, the solvent was evaporated in a rotating oil vacuum evaporator and the residue was then purified via silica column chromatography using the mixture of dichloromethane/methanol (50:1, v/v) as eluent to collect the subfraction of CDs-O-PDOB probe with dark orange color.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparations for the measurements of spectra
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-O-PDOB probe's spectral responses to ONOO− were investigated and all spectrometry measurements were carried out in an aqueous buffered solution and ONOO− was freshly prepared (more details can be found in the Electronic Supportive Information). 20 μL of the CDs-O-PDOB mother solution (1 mg/mL) was added to 2 mL of pH = 7.4 phosphate buffered solution (PBS), then different concentrations of ONOO− were added for 20 min to conduct spectrofluorometric titrations. For pH effects measurements, 20 μL of CDs-O-PDOB was added to 2 mL of freshly prepared PBS with varied pH values. For selectivity test measurements, 20 μL of CDs-O-PDOB was added to 2 mL of pH = 7.4 PBS, then 25 μM ONOO− with 50 μM from various analyst solutions were added respectively for 20 min. The fluorescence emission spectra were measured at λex/em = 498/527 nm and each experiment was repeated three times.

The cytotoxicity of CDs-O-PDOB to living cells was evaluated by CCK-8. Briefly, RAW 264.7 cells were cultured using high glucose DMEM medium (15% FBS) in 96 well plates in incubator for 24 h. After that, the medium was exchanged by fresh medium and each well was incubated with a range of CDs-O-PDOB concentrations (0–50 μg/mL) for 12 h. Then, the cells were rinsed three times with PBS and 10 mL of CCK-8 reagent was added and cultured for 2 h. Finally, 100 μL DMSO was added to dissolve the formed formazan crystal. The plates were stirred for 10 min, and then the absorbance of each well was measured at 450 nm using a Thermo Scientific reader. Each concentration was tested in three replicates and the mean ± standard deviation of the data was used to evaluate cells viability.

The RAW 264.7 cells were treated in high glucose DMEM and seeded on 24-well plates which were placed in an incubator at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. The DMEM was appended with 15% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 100 U·mL-1 penicillin, and 100 μg mL−1 streptomycin. In the first group of experiments, ONOO− is exogenously detected by pretreating the cells with 10 μg/mL CDs-O-PDOB probe for 20 min and then incubated with an increasing amount (0, 125, 250, 500 μM) of SIN-1. In the control group, the scavengers were mixed with 500 μM SIN-1. In the second group of experiments, ONOO− is endogenously detected by culturing the cells with 1 μg/mL LPS and 50 ng/mL IFN-γ for 24 h and then with 10 nM PMA for 30 min; after that incubated with 10 μg/mL CDs-O-PDOB probe for another 20 min. In the control group, the scavengers were mixed with LPS and IFN-γ. The cells of all groups were rinsed with sterile PBS (pH = 7.4) three times and the ﬂuorescence images were collected with Cytation 5 at green and bright channels.

Zebrafish larvae were obtained from Nanjing EzaRinka Biotechnology Co., Ltd and raised in E3 media with PTU at 28 °C. After 5 days post fertilization (dpf), zebrafish were cultured in 6-well plates with 10 μg/mL CDs-O-PDOB probe for 20 min and then incubated with various concentrations (0, 250, 500 μM) of SIN-1 for another 30 min. In the control group, the scavengers were mixed with 500 μM SIN-1. For imaging, the zebrafish were anesthetized with MS-222 and imaged using a Cytation 5 Cell Imaging Multi-Mode Reader, BioTek.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mouse model of inflammation and in vivo imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Reports of animal experiments state that the “Principles of laboratory animal care” (NIH publication No. 86–23, revised 1985) were followed, and was reviewed and approved by the Laboratory Animal Ethics Committee of Jinzhou Medical University (JZMU). Kunming mouse was purchased from the animal experiment center of JZMU. A 3-week-old Kunming mouse with removed hair was injected subcutaneously on the right side with LPS (200 μL, 1 mg/mL) for 24 h and then with PMA (50 μL, 100 μg/mL) for 2 h. After that, the mouse was anesthetized and CDs-O-PDOB probe (50 μL, 1 mg/mL) was injected on the left and right sides of the mouse for 20 min and then imaged directly through in vivo imaging system (Bruker Image Station System). The excitation light channel was 480 nm, and the collected emission path was 530 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the probe for ONOO− detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to synthesize a sensitive fluorescent probe for ONOO−, considering high fluorescence quantum yield, excellent solubility, stability, and low cytotoxicity, CDs are selected as a fluorophore. It is reported that the fluorescence of CDs can be turned off or on by protecting and deprotecting the phenol group on its surface based on the mechanism of static quenching or photoelectron transfer (PET) [49,52,54]. Br-PDOB as a self-immolation group has been reported to react with ONOO− rapidly and selectively. In view of this knowledge, a new probe (CDs-O-PDOB) was created for ONOO− by protecting the phenolic hydroxyl group of CDs with PDOB group. The response mechanism is shown in (Scheme 1). First, the fluorescence of CDs is quenched by PDOB group. When the probe is treated with ONOO−, the PDOB group will self-immolate and release the free CDs with high fluorescence.

The morphological structure of the prepared CDs was characterized by TEM and XRD. As shown in (Fig. 1 a), the CDs is homogeneously spherical and well dispersed with an average diameter of 2.35 nm (Fig. 1a inset). Moreover, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image illustrated that the CDs has a graphene-like crystalline structure with an interlayer lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) lattice fringes of graphene (Fig. 1a inset) [44]. The XRD pattern of the prepared CDs (Fig. 1b) displayed sharp and tight peaks (2θ = 26° and 27.04°) that were attributed to 002 planes of graphene [65].

The optical properties of the CDs were analyzed by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in (Fig. 1c), there are two absorption peaks of the CDs centered at 326 nm which generated from the π→π* transition of the aromatic region and at 494 nm which is assigned to the n→π* transition of surface functional groups [54]. Furthermore, it can be seen from the fluorescence spectra of CDs shown in (Fig. 1d) that the position of the emission peak at 527 nm did not change when the excitation wavelength gradually increased from 408 nm to 498 nm. These results indicate that the CDs possesses excitation-independent emission behavior and the fluorescence emission is caused by the surface emission mechanism [66]. On the other hand, the fluorescence spectra of CDs-O-PDOB with ONOO− in (Fig. 1e) also showed independent emmission with gradually increased excitation wavelength, and this is consistent with the results shown in (Fig. 1d). The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) for CDs was also measured in advance and it was detected to be 78.2% under excitation at 498 nm wavelength, which is considerably higher than some of reported works [21,46,67] (Fig. S1). The prepared CDs was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to identify its surface composition and chemical state. As shown in (Fig. 1f), the broad absorption bands at ∼3440 and ∼3120 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H [68]. The strong peaks at 1610-1517 cm−1 are attributed to the aromatic CC bond stretching vibrations [69,70], while the weak peak at 1655 cm−1 is attributed to CO bond [71].

The XPS survey spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2 a) shows that it mainly contains three peaks, C1s (285.47 eV), O1s (532.98 eV), and S2p (169.17 eV), having the contents of 74.01%, 23.67%, and 2.33%, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum was resolved into two characteristic peaks, including the C–C/CC and C–O bonds accord with the binding energies of 284.80 eV and 286.69 eV, respectively (Fig. 2b) [52,72]. In the O1s spectrum, three peaks at 532.80, 531.37, and 533.94 eV were ascribed to C–O/O–H, CO, and OC–O, respectively (Fig. 2c) [73]. The S2p spectrum can be deconvoluted into SO (169.37 eV), C–S (168.30 eV), S2p3/2 (163.90 eV), and S2p1/2 bonds (165.19 eV) (Fig. 2d) [74].

The XRD pattern of CDs-O-PDOB showed a peak (2θ = 26°) corresponding to that of the free CDs which verify the presence of the carbon core in the probe. The broad peak and the difference between probe and CDs may be due to the low crystallinity of CDs-O-PDOB and the introduction of PDOB groups (Fig. 1b). The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) for CDs-O-PDOB was measured and it was 7.51% which is much lower than that of the free CDs due to the introduction of the quenching PDOB groups to the structure of the CDs (Fig. S1). The FT-IR spectra of CDs-O-PDOB and PDOB were shown in (Fig. 1f). The strength of the broad peak >3000 cm−1 of CDs-O-PDOB decreased, indicating that the phenolic hydroxyl has reacted with PDOB. The strong peaks around 1600 cm−1 are assigned to the aromatic core of CDs. The absorption bands at 2977-2928 cm−1 of CDs-O-PDOB spectra are assigned to the stretching vibrations of aliphatic C–H bonds of PDOB group which confirm the conjugation of CDs and PDOB [75]. Notably, the strong bands of CDs-O-PDOB at 1360–1020 and 1150-1085 cm−1 are assigned to B–O/B–C and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively [76,77].

From the XPS survey spectrum of CDs-O-PDOB (Fig. 3 a), it can be seen that it contains a new element, B1s (191.25 eV), with a content of 2.48% besides the main elements of the CDs (C1s, O1s and S2p) (Fig. 3b–d). The B1s high-resolution spectrum shows two peaks ascribed to B–C and B–O at (191.20 eV) and (192.6 eV), respectively (Fig. 3e) [76]. In addition, it is seen that the conjugation of PDOB with CDs causes an increase in the C/O ratio in which the contents of C1s and O1s became 82.54% and 13.01%, respectively (Figs. 2a and 3 a). The above results clearly demonstrate that the CDs and PDOB are conjugated and led to the formation of the new structure of CDs-O-PDOB.

The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of CDs and CDs-O-PDOB were also investigated and shown in (Fig. 3f and Table S1). The decay curves of both CDs and CDs-O-PDOB are well fitted by double-exponential decay with average photoluminescence (PL) lifetimes of 3.9 ns (τ0) and 3.5 ns (τ) under 498 nm excitation wavelength, respectively. The fluorescence of the CDs-O-PDOB was very weak (QY = 7.51%) compared with that of the CDs (QY = 78.2%). This result is consistent with the reference and maybe because the PDOB group has an electron withdrawing group and had quenched the fluorescence of the CDs through the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [61,78]. Theoretically, static quenching occurs when τ0/τ = 1; whereas dynamic quenching occurs when F0/F = τ0/τ [79]. In this article, the τ0/τ > 1 and the F0/F ≠ τ0/τ, which articulates that both static quenching and dynamic quenching occur in this process. Because the τ is close to τ0, the static quenching effect was likely predominant in this system. Besides, there are two ways of dynamic quenching: PET and energy transfer. (Fig. 1c) illustrates that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of PDOB group does not overlap with the emission spectrum of CDs, which elucidates that the quenching of the fluorescence is not caused by energy transfer [80]. From these results we can conclude that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of PDOB group to CDs is mainly assigned to static quenching and photo-induced electron transfer effect in CDs-O-PDOB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent responses of CDs-O-PDOB toward ONOO−
Time and pH to fluorescence responses of CDs-O-PDOB
ce:section.ce:para: The time-dependence response of CDs-O-PDOB toward ONOO− was investigated (Fig. 4 a). At 0 μM ONOO−, it can be seen that there is almost no obvious fluorescence signal enhancement within 60 min, as well as at 2 μM ONOO− was very slight. When at 10, 50 and 120 μM of ONOO−, the fluorescence intensities of CDs-O-PDOB had a moderate enhancement. All the solutions reached a plateau value within 20 min. Meanwhile, the resistance to photobleaching was determined after CDs-O-PDOB incubated with 50 μM ONOO− for 60 min (Fig. S2). The fluorescence emission intensity did not show significant decrease after continuous light irradiation for 180 min. This result shows that this ONOO− detecting system has good photostability.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence was also investigated. The probe itself kept consistently low fluorescence in the pH range from 5 to 10 (Fig. 4b). On the other hand, the solution of CDs-O-PDOB with ONOO− displayed obvious fluorescence enhancement from pH = 7 to 11. Thus, the above observations indicate that the detection of ONOO− by CDs-O-PDOB can be performed under physiological conditions and could be potentially relevant for imaging ONOO− in cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence selectivity of CDs-O-PDOB to detect ONOO−
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the specificity of CDs-O-PDOB for detecting ONOO−, the reactivity of the probe with biologically active species, including amino acids (Arg, Asp, Glu, His, Val, Ser, Thr, Trp, Lys, Gly, Tyr, and Phe), RSS (GSH, Cys, Hcy, and NaHS), RNS/ROS (ONOO−, H2O2, NaClO, NaHCO3, Na2HPO4, •OH, and ROO•), and other ions (Fe3+, K+, I−, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Br−, NO2 −, and CO3 2−) was examined. After adding 25 μM ONOO− and 50 μM analysts into the PBS solution with CDs-O-PDOB, ONOO− could distinctly enhance the fluorescence of the probe (Fig. 4d), indicating that CDs-O-PDOB possesses an excellent selectivity towards ONOO−. Previous reports indicated that the probe based on phenylborate ester and phenylborate group is also sensitive to H2O2 and ClO−, and we carefully checked the selectivity of the probe [81]. As shown in (Fig. 4d and f), although 50 μM H2O2 and ClO− cause a limited increase in fluorescence (6.2 and 2.2 times), but 25 μM ONOO− promoted a 22-fold significant fluorescence enhancement. Taking into consideration the low biological content of ClO− and H2O2 (from picomol to micromol) and the limited fluorescence increase induced by them, the probe can still detect ONOO− well without interference from other ROS. To estimate the sensitivity of this sensing probe to ONOO− detection, the fluorescence responses towards the potential interferences from the coexisting biomolecules were also investigated under the optimal experimental conditions with the results shown in (Fig. 4d and e), wherein it illustrates that the above mentioned coexisting biomolecules pose no apparent interfering effects on the detection of ONOO−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectra of CDs-O-PDOB towards ONOO−
ce:section.ce:para: In spectrofluorometric titrations, CDs-O-PDOB showed relatively weak fluorescence when excited at 498 nm. However, it exhibited a strong emission band centered at 527 nm upon treatment with different concentrations of ONOO− (Fig. 4c). Moreover, the fluorescence titration assay showed a dose-dependent increment in fluorescence intensities, which accomplished saturation at 120 μM ONOO− (Fig. 4c inset) and a preferable linear correlation (R 2 = 0.9985) between the fluorescence intensities and ONOO− concentrations from 0 to 10 μM was attained with a calculated limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.26 μM based on 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurement, and k is the linear slope of fluorescence intensity of CDs-O-PDOB with ONOO− concentrations (Fig. S3). Compared with some of formerly reported fluorescent probes for detecting ONOO− (Table S2), our probe has a similar detection limit and linear range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms for fluorescence response of CDs-O-PDOB to ONOO−
ce:section.ce:para: The boronate-based self-immolated group (PDOB) was covalently attached to the phenol hydroxyl of CDs, which can highly efficiently and quickly react with strong Lewis bases ONOO− [62]. After the self-immolated linker break through 1,4- immolation, the CDs was released and the green fluorescence of the CDs was restored (Scheme 2 ). Because the composition of CDs is complex and the precise structure is unclear, thin layer chromatography (TLC) was used to prove this process [54]. As shown in (Fig. S4), the polarity of CDs-O-PDOB (Rf = 0.69) is much lower than the green fluorescent components in CDs (Rf = 0.14 and 0.42), because the phenolic hydroxyl group with high polarity in green CDs is protected and the ether bond with low polarity is formed. After reaction with ONOO−, the spots corresponding to CDs-O-PDOB disappeared and the spots corresponding to CDs reappeared. This can also be confirmed from the fluorescence spectra of the CDs and CDs-O-PDOB with ONOO−. According to (Fig. 1e), the fluorescence intensities of CDs-O-PDOB with ONOO− at different excitation wavelengths from 408 nm to 498 nm compared with that of CDs (Fig. 1d) also showed that the emission peaks centered at 527 nm as expected and further confirm that the compound resulted from the reaction of CDs-O-PDOB and ONOO− is the CDs and the fluorescence emission is caused by the surface emission mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of ONOO− in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the desirable fluorescence results above, the potential of CDs-O-PDOB probe for visualizing exogenous and endogenous ONOO− in living cells was evaluated. Firstly, the safety of CDs-O-PDOP was precisely studied by CCK-8 assay method (Fig. S5). The results showed that the probe exhibited low cytotoxicity (cell viability >80% probe) when treated with 0–50 μM CDs-O-PDOB which can be predicted that it is a probe with good biocompatibility and suitable for cell imaging experiments. For the detection of exogenous ONOO− in living cells, RAW 264.7 cells were pretreated with 10 μg/mL CDs-O-PDOB probe for 20 min and then incubated with increasing amounts (0,125, 250, 500 μM) of 3-morpholino-sydnonimine (SIN-1, a recognized ONOO− donor) for 30 min [82,83]. As illustrated in (Fig. 5 a–d), the fluorescence intensities progressively increased as the concentration of SIN-1 increased. When the cells were pretreated with 500 μM SIN-1 and 300 μM 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinyl-1-oxide (TEMPO, a scavenger for superoxide radical) [84], barely no fluorescence change was observed because of the impeded ONOO− involvement (Fig. 5e). Similarly, the intracellular fluorescence signal was pale when cells were pretreated with 500 μM SIN-1 and 300 μM minocycline (MIC, a scavenger directly for ONOO−) (Fig. 5f) [85]. Furthermore, another group of CDs-O-PDOB labeled cells were pretreated with 500 μM SIN-1 and 1 mM N-acetylcysteine (NAC, a potent ROS scavenger) [15], and the fluorescence signal was weak as expected (Fig. 5g).

It has been reported that endogenous ONOO− can be produced in mouse macrophage cell line RAW 264.7 by the stimulation of lipopolysaccharide (LPS), interferon-γ (IFN-γ), and phorbol-12-myristate-13-acetate (PMA) [16,19,86]. To examine whether CDs-O-PDOB probe can be applied to detect endogenous ONOO− in living cells, RAW 264.7 cells were pretreated with 1 μg/mL LPS and 50 ng/mL IFN-γ for 24 h and then with 10 nM PMA for 30 min; after that incubated with 10 μg/mL CDs-O-PDOB for another 20 min. As illustrated in (Fig. 6 a–b), the cells treated with the stimulants exhibited much brighter fluorescence than those of the blank group. Noteworthy, the morphology of cells in the stimulated group has been altered compared to the blank group and this is due to the addition of ONOO− stimulants. LPS and PMA as reactive oxygen species generators, are also well-known inflammation inducers that led to some alteration in the morphology of cells [87]. As a control, another group of CDs-O-PDOB loaded cells were pretreated with the stimulants together with the well-known ONOO− scavengers (TEMPO, MIC and NAC, respectively), which displayed reduced fluorescence intensity (Fig. 6c–e). These results indicate that CDs-O-PDOB probe is applicable for responding to the exogenous and endogenous ONOO− generation in live cells and suggest its promise for serving as a molecular imaging tool to explore ONOO− under pathological conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging of ONOO− in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the success of CDs-O-PDOB probe in imaging exogenous and endogenous ONOO− in RAW264.7, we then examined the performance of CDs-O-PDOB probe in in vivo imaging of ONOO− in live animals. Initially, zebrafish were selected due to their small body size, transparent body, conserved structures, and diverse biological functions like human [88]. Five days old zebrafish were pretreated with 10 μg/mL CDs-O-PDOB probe for 20 min and then incubated with various concentrations (0, 250, 500 μM) of SIN-1 for 30 min [86]. As shown in (Fig. 7 a–c), the zebrafish treated with SIN-1 showed an increasingly intense fluorescence signal compared with the blank group that showed a faint fluorescence, and this could be ascribed to the existence of ONOO− in zebrafish by the donation of SIN-1 and further confirms the results of the cell imaging experiments. To better elucidate the ability of CDs-O-PDOB to detect ONOO− in zebrafish, the investigation of control groups after adding ONOO− scavengers were established. As it can be seen from (Fig. 7d–f), the fluorescence intensities of the control groups were decreased after adding the scavengers compared to the groups without scavengers. These results illustrate better the ability of CDs-O-PDOB to detect ONOO− in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging of ONOO− in mouse model inflammation
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, CDs-O-PDOB probe was utilized to in vivo imaging of endogenous ONOO− in a living mouse. It is reported that ONOO− is linked to developing numerous inflammatory diseases [10]. To further confirm the relevance between inflammation and ONOO− formation, CDs-O-PDOB probe was utilized to detect ONOO− in an inflamed mouse model. The right side of a Kunming mouse was given a subcutaneous injection of 200 μL of LPS (1 mg/mL) for 24 h and then injected with 50 μL of PMA (100 μg/mL) for 2 h to induce inflammation (Fig. 8 a). After that, 50 μL of CDs-O-PDOB (1 mg/mL) was subcutaneously injected into the left and right sides of the mouse for 20 min and then imaged. As shown in (Fig. 8b–e), we can see that the inflamed leg showed conspicuous fluorescence, and the normal leg showed weak fluorescence. This result was coordinated with the results of cell imaging and in vivo zebrafish imaging discussed above, indicating that CDs-O-PDOB probe displays a good detection and imaging of inflammation-associated ONOO− in living animals.

In summary, a new fluorescent probe (CDs-O-PDOB) based on CDs for ONOO− detection was successfully designed and synthesized. The characterization of CDs and CDs-O-PDOB and the reaction between the CDs and the recognition unit (PDOB) were verified by HRTEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, UV–vis absorbance, and TRPL analysis. The presence of a PDOB group in the probe molecule gave it great potential to be linked to ONOO− and led to the release of the CDs fluorescence. The new probe exhibited favorable recognition properties, included with a low limit of detection, rapid response, low cytotoxicity, and high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of exogenous and endogenous ONOO− in RAW 264.7 cells. Furthermore, the probe possessed excellent detection properties for in vivo imaging of ONOO− in zebrafish and mouse model of inflammation. We believe that this work came up with an opportune imaging tool to demonstrate the biological roles of ONOO− in physiological and pathological conditions.
